skill.name: harden
skill.description: Improve interface resilience through better error handling, i18n support, text overflow handling, and edge case management. Makes interfaces robust and production-ready.
skill.summary: Systematically harden interfaces against edge cases, internationalization issues, errors, and real-world usage scenarios
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 34 potential issues but all are false positives from markdown code blocks and comments. The skill contains only educational documentation with CSS/JS code examples. No executable code, network calls, file system access, or external commands present. This is a purely instructional skill about UI hardening best practices.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analyzer incorrectly flagged markdown code fences (```) and CSS/JS code examples as external commands and weak cryptography. These are documentation code blocks, not executable code. Lines 50-311 contain CSS, JSX, and JavaScript examples in markdown code fences that triggered pattern-based detection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Directly observed all flagged content - all are markdown code blocks and comments, no executable commands present

content.user_title: Harden Your Interfaces Against Production Reality
content.value_statement: Interfaces break when real users enter unexpected data, lose connection, or use assistive technologies. This skill systematically strengthens your UI against edge cases, internationalization issues, errors, and accessibility gaps to ensure production-ready resilience.
actual_capabilities: Systematically identify UI weaknesses through extreme input testing (very long text, emoji, RTL languages, large datasets)
Implement text overflow handling with CSS truncation, line clamping, and flex/grid overflow prevention
Add internationalization support including text expansion budgets, RTL layout support, and proper date/number formatting
Create comprehensive error handling for network failures, API errors, and form validation with recovery options
Build accessibility resilience with keyboard navigation, screen reader support, and motion sensitivity considerations
Apply performance resilience patterns including debouncing, memory leak prevention, and progressive loading
limitations: Does not automatically fix existing code - provides guidance and patterns for manual implementation
Cannot test all possible edge cases automatically - requires manual testing with real data
Framework-agnostic advice that may need adaptation for specific UI frameworks (React, Vue, Angular)
Does not replace comprehensive accessibility audits - provides patterns but not full WCAG compliance verification

use_cases.title: Frontend Developer Preparing for Production Launch
use_cases.description: A developer has built a feature that works in development but needs to ensure it handles real-world scenarios like slow networks, long translated text, and error states before deploying to production.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developer preparing UI for production

use_cases.title: Team Internationalizing Application for Global Markets
use_cases.description: A product team is expanding to German, Arabic, and Japanese markets and needs to ensure their UI handles text expansion, right-to-left layouts, and different character sets without breaking.

use_cases.title: UX Engineer Improving Error Experience
use_cases.description: A UX specialist wants to move beyond generic error messages to create helpful, actionable error states that guide users through recovery with clear next steps.
use_cases.target_user: UX engineer focused on error states

prompt_templates.title: Harden specific component
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my user profile form and harden it against edge cases. Check for text overflow issues, add proper error handling with retry options, ensure it works with German translations (30% longer), and verify keyboard navigation works.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner - Get help hardening a specific UI component

prompt_templates.title: Internationalization audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my dashboard layout for internationalization readiness. Identify fixed widths that will break with German text, check if date formatting uses Intl API, verify RTL support with logical properties, and test emoji handling in data fields.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate - Prepare UI for multi-language support

prompt_templates.title: Error state hardening
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my API integration and add comprehensive error handling. Handle 400/401/403/404/429/500 status codes appropriately, add retry logic with backoff, preserve user input on validation errors, and create offline fallback states.

prompt_templates.title: Accessibility and performance hardening
prompt_templates.prompt: Harden my data table for accessibility and performance. Add keyboard navigation, ARIA labels for screen readers, virtual scrolling for 10000+ rows, debounced search input, and test with Windows high contrast mode.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced - Combine accessibility with performance patterns

output_examples.input: Review this button component and identify hardening issues: <button className='w-24'>Submit</button>
output: Fixed width (w-24) will break when translated to German (Submit → Absenden, 30% longer)
No loading state - user could double-click on slow connections
No error handling - if API call fails, button gives no feedback
No keyboard focus indication for accessibility

output_examples.input: How should I handle long usernames in my profile card?
output: Use CSS truncation for single-line overflow: .truncate { overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; white-space: nowrap; }
Add full username in title attribute for hover tooltip
Test with 100+ character names from directories
Consider expanding to full name on click/hover for better UX
Ensure flex container has min-width: 0 to allow shrinking

output_examples.input: My date picker breaks with Arabic text. What should I check?
output: Use Intl.DateTimeFormat with 'ar-SA' locale for proper Arabic date formatting
Switch to CSS logical properties: margin-inline-start instead of margin-left
Add dir='rtl' attribute or test with [dir='rtl'] CSS selector
Verify layout mirrors correctly for RTL (padding-inline, border-inline)
Test with actual Arabic text not just pseudo-translations to catch character set issues

best_practices: Always test with extreme data: 100+ character strings, emoji, RTL languages (Arabic/Hebrew), CJK characters, and completely empty states to catch overflow and encoding issues
Use CSS logical properties (margin-inline, padding-inline) instead of physical properties (margin-left, padding-right) to automatically support RTL layouts
Add 30-40% text expansion space budget for translations, especially when designing in English since German and Finnish can be significantly longer
anti_patterns: Never use fixed widths (w-64, w-48) on text containers - they break when translated to longer languages like German or Finnish
Don't assume English pluralization rules work globally - use proper i18n libraries that handle languages with complex plural forms like Polish or Arabic
Avoid generic error messages like 'Error occurred' - always explain what happened and provide specific next steps like 'Check your connection' or 'Try again'

faq.question: Why does my layout break when translated to German?
faq.answer: German text is typically 30% longer than English. Fixed-width containers designed for English text overflow when translated. Solution: Use flexible layouts (flexbox, grid) with padding instead of fixed widths, and add 30-40% expansion space budget during design.

faq.question: How do I test for edge cases efficiently?
faq.answer: Create a test data set with extreme values: 100+ character strings, empty strings, emoji (including skin tone variants), RTL text (Arabic/Hebrew), CJK characters, and very large numbers. Test each field manually with this data, then add automated tests for critical paths.

faq.question: Should I harden during development or after?
faq.answer: Harden during development. It is faster to build resilience from the start than to retrofit it later. Include edge case testing in your definition of done for each component. Treat hardening as equal in importance to feature implementation.

faq.question: What is the most common UI hardening mistake?
faq.answer: Fixed widths on text containers. This is the top cause of internationalization breakage. Always use flexbox/grid with padding or min-width instead of fixed width. Set min-width: 0 on flex/grid items to allow them to shrink below their content size.

faq.question: How do I handle text overflow in flex containers?
faq.answer: Add min-width: 0 to flex items to allow them to shrink below their content size, then add overflow: hidden and text-overflow: ellipsis for truncation. The min-width: 0 is critical - without it, flex items refuse to shrink smaller than their content.

faq.question: Do I need to test every language I support?
faq.answer: Test with your longest language (usually German or Finnish) and one RTL language (Arabic or Hebrew). These catch the majority of i18n layout issues. Also test with CJK characters for encoding issues, but you do not need to test every translation comprehensively.
